topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — Kim & Chang (2018)
change_summary: detail_evidence shrank from 4207 to 2330 chars (45% reduction) since c4 review. Decider applied c4 recommendations: removed cascade paragraphs 2-4, replaced with one-line cross-reference to Section 1.8. Removed off-topic 2026 predictions paragraph. WIN-010 and WIN-025 also patched with cascade cross-references (fixing c4 hole 2). Triple-counting phrasing unchanged. Post-hoc dating now includes nine-year gap.
our_argument_summary: Verdict is Std Model Explains. The argument has three pillars: (1) Chapman ionospheric Sq mechanism (1933) predicts the full ensemble signal from first principles; (2) the dome provides no derivation from its geometry — only a post-hoc scaling formula (FSF) empirically fitted to station data; (3) triple-counting: WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 are three entries for one phenomenon. A subsidiary cross-reference to Section 1.8 (refraction cascade) targets the dome's own mechanism without contaminating the verdict framing.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py available in raw-text dump. The dome's 02-wins.txt lists PROS-003 (Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly Tracks Geometry) referencing BOU 2017 and 9-station 2024 data, but the 39-eclipse ensemble (Kim & Chang 2018) is not explicitly tracked as automation — it is a retrospective literature citation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — no automation exists for the ensemble. The dome declares it confirmed without any code-side validation.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None. The WIN is asserted qualitatively with no numeric tolerance to breach.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our claim that monitoring="none" is correct — the dome has no automation for the 39-eclipse ensemble.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py code exists for the 39-eclipse ensemble. Kim & Chang (2018) is a historical meta-analysis. The dome's PROS-003 tracks BOU 2017 and 2024 9-station data, not the Kim & Chang ensemble. Tag: none.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) + Sq current framework (Yamazaki & Maute 2021) predict eclipse magnetic perturbations from UV ionization reduction. Dome relabels as "aetheric field perturbation by geometric shadow" — same phenomenon, different vocabulary, no new quantitative prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang published in 2018; dome incorporated it in V51.0 (early 2026) — an 8-year gap. No pre-registration of expected values before the data was available.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation from disc_radius, firmament_height, or dome geometry produces the component patterns, latitude dependence, or 180-minute timescale observed. The dome's FSF formula is calibrated against data, not derived from geometry.

holes_found.description: Triple-counting phrasing in finding still includes WIN-068 in its own list: "triple-counts the same phenomenon as WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068." This reads as WIN-068 counting itself. The c4 review flagged this (hole 3) and recommended rephrasing to clarify that WIN-068 duplicates the basis already counted in WIN-010 and WIN-025. The finding has not been updated.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase finding to: "Non-discriminating: Chapman (1933) ionospheric mechanism predicts eclipse magnetic perturbations across all 39 eclipses. Dome adds no equation; this entry duplicates the observational basis already counted as WIN-010 and WIN-025 — the ensemble is a larger aggregation of the same phenomenon."

holes_found.description: Meza et al. 2021 citation in detail_verdict_text paragraph 1 ("Chapman 1933; Meza et al. 2021") lacks full bibliographic details — no journal, volume, or DOI. All other citations in this WIN have full references (Kim & Chang with journal/volume/pages, Chapman with journal/volume, Yamazaki & Maute with URL). The incomplete citation was flagged in c4 and remains unfixed.
holes_found.recommendation: Add full reference: Meza et al. (2021) likely refers to a paper on ionospheric eclipse effects; verify the exact reference and add journal/pages. If the citation cannot be verified, remove it — Chapman (1933) alone is sufficient as the foundational reference.

holes_found.description: Post-hoc paragraph says "nine-year gap" but the actual gap is 8 years (Kim & Chang published 2018; V51.0 is early 2026 = ~8 years). Off by one year. A dome defender could nitpick this. Minor because the qualitative point (many years gap) is unaffected.
holes_found.recommendation: Change "nine-year gap" to "eight-year gap" or "nearly a decade.". The latter is both accurate and rhetorically stronger.

holes_found.description: The cascade cross-reference in paragraph 2 of detail_evidence says "the refraction cascade makes the physical sun position seasonally indeterminate across the 39-eclipse ensemble." This is concise and appropriate as a subordinate cross-reference. However, the phrasing "seasonally indeterminate" is somewhat opaque for a reader who hasn't read Section 1.8 yet. The purpose of the cross-reference is to invite the reader to explore; clarity helps.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider: "For a separate internal-consistency concern — the dome's refraction fix leaves the physical sun altitude indeterminate, undermining the geometric shadow it invokes for all 39 eclipses — see Section 1.8." Slightly longer but self-contained enough to hook the reader.

stronger_arguments.description: The 30-50 degree magnetic latitude peak (Kim & Chang 2018 Fig. 4) is a specific consequence of the Sq current vortex geometry in the dipole geomagnetic field. The dome's flat-disc coordinate system has no physical mechanism to produce a peak at this latitude band. This is a strong discriminator we mention in paragraph 3 of detail_evidence ("strongest at Sq-vortex latitudes ~30-50 degrees") but we don't explicitly contrast it with what the dome predicts — which is nothing. A sentence saying "the dome offers no explanation for why 30-50 degrees is special" would sharpen the argument.

The dome's own Steelman Evidence Ladder placing eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1 ("consistent with both models") is already mentioned, but it could be promoted as a structural argument. If the dome's own classification says this evidence is non-discriminating, then claiming it as a WIN contradicts their own assessment framework. This is not just "we say it's non-discriminating" — the dome says so too.
sources: Dome Steelman Evidence Ladder (raw-text/02-wins.txt or dome site)

kernel_of_truth.description: Kim & Chang (2018) is genuine peer-reviewed science showing a robust, repeatable 39-eclipse magnetic signal. The dome is correct that eclipse geomagnetic perturbations are real, statistically significant, and systematically patterned. The instinct to aggregate from single-station (WIN-010) to ensemble (WIN-068) is methodologically sound — more data is better.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The observation's reality is not in dispute. The question is discriminatory power: does this observation distinguish dome cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics? Chapman (1933) + modern Sq models predict the exact same pattern from first principles using only ionospheric physics and no dome parameters. The dome contributes no equation, no different predicted magnitude, and no different latitude dependence. A genuine dome prediction would differ quantitatively from the standard model — this one does not.

deeper_analysis: The c4 holes are substantially addressed. The cascade paragraphs were removed and replaced with a clean cross-reference (c4 hole 1, option a — applied). WIN-010 and WIN-025 now also have cascade cross-references (c4 hole 2 — fixed). The off-topic 2026 predictions paragraph was removed (c4 hole 4 — fixed). Post-hoc dating now specifies V51.0 and the year gap (c4 hole 6 — partially fixed, but says "nine-year" when it should be "eight-year"). The triple-counting phrasing (c4 hole 3) and incomplete Meza citation (c4 unchecked citation) remain unaddressed — both moderate/minor. Overall the text is now much tighter and internally consistent. The verdict Std Model Explains is well-supported by three independent arguments: Chapman mechanism priority, FSF empirical fitting (not derivation), and triple-counting. The confidence increase from 0.78 to 0.88 reflects the successful cleanup.

citations_verified: Chapman 1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3 — standard historical reference, widely cited
Kim & Chang 2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054 — volume/page plausible for ASR 2018
citations_unchecked: Yamazaki & Maute 2021 Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences — URL format correct (10.3389/fspas.2021.766327), not retrieved to verify content
Meza et al. 2021 — no journal/volume/page given; cannot verify without more detail

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender says: "Your review admits the observation is real and the ensemble is methodologically sound. You claim Chapman (1933) explains it, but Chapman's original model was semi-quantitative — it did not predict the specific ~10 nT magnitude, the component pattern (Y+, X/Z/F-), or the 30-50 degree latitude peak. Those specific predictions came from later refinements of ionospheric models that were themselves calibrated against eclipse observations. So your 'standard physics predicts it from first principles' is also post-hoc calibration — just done by mainstream scientists instead of the dome author. The dome's FSF formula and the standard model's Sq current parameters both require empirical fitting. You hold the dome to a standard of pure derivation that standard physics itself does not meet for this specific phenomenon." This defense has some force because Chapman 1933 was indeed qualitative, and modern Sq predictions do incorporate empirical ionospheric parameters.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add a sentence to detail_evidence acknowledging that modern Sq predictions also involve empirical ionospheric parameters, but distinguishing them from the dome's FSF: "While modern Sq current models incorporate measured ionospheric parameters (electron density profiles, conductivity tensors), these parameters are independently measured by ionosondes and satellites — not circularly derived from the eclipse signal itself. The dome's FSF, by contrast, is fitted directly to the eclipse observations it claims to predict." This preempts the "both sides calibrate" attack.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Resolved since c4. WIN-010 and WIN-025 now both include cascade cross-references to Section 1.8, aligning with WIN-068's treatment. All three eclipse-magnetic WINs have: (a) Std Model Explains verdict, (b) Chapman mechanism as primary argument, (c) subordinate cascade cross-reference to Section 1.8. WIN-012 (magnetic-gravity coupling constant 1.67 nT/uGal) uses the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT value from WIN-010; this is consistent — our critique of the coupling constant does not depend on whether the -10.9 nT itself is dome-derived or standard-model-explained.

claims_checked: FSF range 0.642-2.075 => ratio 3.23x (text says 3.2x — correct to 1 d.p.)
Kim & Chang 2018 ASR volume 61 pages 2040-2054 (volume/pages plausible)
Post-hoc gap: 2018 publication to V51.0 (early 2026) = 8 years (text says nine — off by one)
Chapman 1933 journal ref: Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity vol 38 p 3 (standard citation)
quantitative_verification.computations: FSF ratio: 2.075/0.642 = 3.232 ✓. Year gap: 2026-2018 = 8 years (text says "nine-year gap" — minor error). All other numerical claims match.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The post-hoc gap is described as "nine-year" but the arithmetic gives 8 years (Kim & Chang 2018 → V51.0 early 2026). Minor numerical imprecision.
